secsa__001_a.text:
Research question
What are the components of an intervention to embed trauma-informed practices responsive to the
needs of survivors of sexual violence and abuse in emergency departments (EDs)?
Background
Sexual violence has lifelong impacts on survivors’ physical and mental health, is associated with
significant health inequalities and very costly to society. Survivors often avoid healthcare (e.g. cancer
screening) due to prior negative encounters with services and fears about re-traumatisation. EDs are a
vital entry point to the NHS, but the setting (e.g. urgency of care, fast-paced environment) creates
significant access challenges for trauma survivors. Among the 25 million annual ED attendances in
England, over 4m are estimated to be survivors of lifetime sexual violence. Current ED practices may
aggravate distress, self-discharge, and poor adherence to advice. Staff report feeling underprepared to
support survivors. Trauma-informed care can transform the healthcare encounter by prioritising safety,
trust, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. However, there is limited evidence on introducing these
practices into EDs.
Aims and objectives
This study aims to co-design a complex

secsa__001_b.text:
, trauma-informed intervention to improve ED care for survivors
of sexual trauma.
Objectives:
1. Examine lived experiences of survivors accessing ED care and staff who provide the care and
prioritise what needs to change at the patient-clinician interface, and for health professionals, the
environment and organisation.
2. Identify ‘functions’ of the planned intervention
3. Co-design intervention components
4. Develop an underpinning theory of change.
Methods
Integrating Experience-Based Co-Design with a behaviour change framework, survivors and staff will
come together to co-design an intervention. Arts-based methods will underpin discovery, co-produced
content and knowledge transfer. Partner organisation, Survivors Voices, will lead on work with people
and communities, provide peer support and expertly facilitate co-design groups to engage survivors.
Phase 1 M1-16 Identify the need for change using focus groups with NHS staff (n=20), and narrative
interviews with survivors (n=30) recruited in health and community. We will gather narratives and
‘touchpoints’ of ED care to form the basis of arts-based outputs and prioritise targets for change.
Phase 2 M17-18 Define intervention func

secsa__001_c.text:
tions using a co-design event with a mixed subset of 20
participants.
Phase 3 M19-24 Co-develop prototypes for the intervention in 3 mixed sub-groups with 15 participants,
such as skills training, resources tailored to the ED, local agreed referral pathways.
Impact and mobilisation
Findings to be shared widely in accessible formats with community and survivor organisations and key
professional bodies e.g. RCEM and RCN. This work will provide a foundation for quality improvement,
healthcare policy, ED service design, NHS efficiencies and better health and healthcare for survivors.
We will refine a theory of change and prepare for the next stage, to address uncertainties and
demonstrate promise in a feasibility study.
Inclusion
Boosting our work with survivors and communities, an inclusion strategy will expand reach to
underserved communities at the intersection of a high prevalence of sexual violence, poor health and
marginalisation. Actions include geographically and demographically diverse NHS sites, local
champions, and bespoke support for participation.

secpesor__001_a.text:
What’s this study about?
This study is about improving emergency department care for people who have experienced sexual
violence and abuse.
Why it matters
Sexual violence causes deep and lasting harm to survivors, their families, and communities. More than
10 million people in Britain have experienced sexual violence as children or adults. Sexual violence
affects physical, sexual, and mental health. But visiting the GP, having screening tests or attending
hospital can feel uncomfortable, even distressing for survivors. Survivors may worry about not having a
say in what happens to them or their bodies, being dismissed or blamed for their difficulties. They may
re-live feelings or memories connected with abuse. Many survivors avoid healthcare altogether which
can make health worse.
Emergency departments are especially difficult for survivors – the care is urgent and unavoidable. The
environment is busy, noisy, and very clinical. Emergency departments could be more survivor-centred –
like offering greater choice, showing care in asking about abuse, or for consent before examinations or
treatment, and taking steps to help a person feel safe. These changes could improve fairness and
acc
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
essibility, survivors’ health and lower NHS costs.
What this study will do?
We will plan a new approach to care for survivors. We will do this using a type of research called
‘Experience-Based Co-Design’. Survivors will be invited to share about times they went to the
emergency department. NHS staff will tell about caring for survivors as patients. Using what survivors
and staff tell us, we will work with survivor-artists to create powerful outputs, including a short film. Next,
survivors and staff will work together to agree goals for the new approach for emergency departments.
For example, a goal might be to improve NHS staff understanding about the health impacts of sexual
violence or create a safer environment. Finally, smaller groups of participants will plan how to make
changes, like showing staff how to communicate concerns about abuse.
Survivors and community leaders were involved in planning the research to date, especially on including
survivors from a range of communities, safely and fairly. They advised that arts-based methods are an
excellent way to share about research and that health services can be places of healing over distress. It
is vital survivors continue to c
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
o-lead this work.
What will we do with the results?
We will set out a new approach to emergency care that allows survivors get the healthcare they need.
We will plan a follow-on study, looking at whether the approach could lead to better outcomes for
survivors, staff and the NHS. We will explore if it might be possible and sensible to roll-out to emergency
departments nationwide in the future. Creative outputs will explain what we did and learnt.
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

seccfps__001_a.text:
We thank the committee for their review and constructive comments. Please find below responses to the
points raised.
1. The rationale of proposed study was unclear...clarify how arts-based and creative outputs would feed
into intervention development and what final intervention might be.
We included stronger rationale based on gap in evidence for transforming EDs from places of distress
into environments of healing and empowerment for survivors, and a policy impetus to reshape EDs for
better sexual violence and mental health care in the future.
Arts-based methods are central to the participatory approach needed to achieve the aims. Endorsed by
PPI, creative outputs (e.g. 5-min film reflecting stakeholders’ needs-in-context) register the collective
effort of staff and survivors. They feed into intervention development by 1) stimulating co-design work 2)
being integrated into the intervention (e.g. to impart skills, educate, stimulate action) and 3) sharing key
messages in ways that engage and are accessible.
The final intervention is based on priorities identified, existing theory/evidence and the co-designed
functions/features. It will include elements to target change at the patie
seccfps__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_b.text:
nt-clinician interface, healthcare
professionals’ practice, environment and systems e.g. skills building, patient resources, referral
pathways (see Figure 1, Detailed Research Plan).
2. Concerns raised on aspects of research plan and methodology.
(i) Provide more information on how participants would be recruited, taking into account safeguarding,
particularly if participants would be self-identifying as survivors
To ensure safety (see Box 3, main application) we will model trauma-informed principles in the research
process and prioritise participants’ physical and emotional safety and consider needs of stakeholders,
recipients of project outputs and the team. The PPI partner (Survivors Voices) has 20 years’ experience
of working for ‘a safer society with support for survivors that is survivor-sensitive, empowering and
services that are trauma-responsive’. The inclusion lead (Little Ro) brings deep expertise in safely
engaging survivors from marginalised settings (e.g. with Black and people of colour communities).
Key safeguards include:
• Careful sampling/recruitment, check individual readiness, peer support
• Engage NHS site safeguarding teams in set-up
• Up-to-date resources, sa
seccfps__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_c.text:
fe follow-up
• Responsiveness to risk/signs of domestic abuse, mental health concerns, mandatory reporting
requirements
• Maximise participant choice and agency
• Clear boundaries for group work and use survivor-led facilitation
• Responsive to staff needs e.g. workplace pressures, abuse, personal trauma.
(ii) Outline number/type of sites involved
There will be 2 socio-economically differentiated sites: University Hospitals Coventry & Warwickshire
NHS Trust (UHCW) and South Warwickshire University NHS Foundation Trust (SWFT). Both sites are
Type 1 EDs - one is a major trauma centre (UHCW) and one is a local emergency hospital (SWFT).
(iii) Clarify claim the intervention would be multi-level
Recognising that outcomes are shaped by complex, interacting factors across a complex healthcare
system, and consistent with existing research on transformative care for abuse, the intervention would
target change at the levels of patient experience, healthcare professionals’ practice, environment and
systems.
(iv) Address concerns about feasibility and sustainability of the proposed approach, given staff turnover
and environment in EDs
Feasibility longer-term will be established in future testi
seccfps__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

seccfps__001_d.text:
ng and exploring implementation and scale-up in
the NHS. High staff turnover, pace and pressure of the ED also present challenges for the early phase
research. All engagements with staff will be done with care recognising unique challenges for this
professional group and physical/psychological trauma as core to the work of the ED. The current
research seeks 108 hours of staff time (activity/travel) split across 2 sites. Senior clinical champions will
lead recruitment with CL, who brings extensive experience in engaging staff as UHCW's lead for
research in the ED. Regular check-ins will be scheduled to pre-empt challenges. CL will identify barriers
to engagement early, offer flexible participation in co-design, provide incentives (vouchers/NHS research
costs for time/travel), highlight benefits from the intervention (e.g. increased confidence, reduced stress,
and enhanced trauma awareness) and creatively celebrate progress.
(v) Time commitment for some co-apps considered insufficient...should be reviewed and justified or
amended keeping in mind the funding limit
The budget attempts to balance having the right time/expertise with sufficient resources for safe,
successful delivery and

outputs. One change was to increase the PPI partner’s (AN) time from 5-13%
for more co-design and knowledge exchange. They bring advocacy, peer support, access to networks
for recruitment and dissemination–all vital for success. CL (5%) will enable staff recruitment,

project efficiencies, governance and support ELW in the day-to day running.
(vi) Committee requested the PES be rewritten to improve accessibility
We have worked extensively with 3 survivor-experts and service leaders to rewrite the summary,
eliminating complex language and simplifying sentences.

secppi__001_a.text:
Our 'Working with People and Communities' summary makes reference to 3 strategies that underpin the
project: (1) Approach to safety; (2) Involvement of survivors as equal partners in the research; and (3)
Inclusive research.
(1) This research relies on the participation of survivors of sexual trauma to advance its goal of
enhancing the capability and commitment of NHS emergency departments (EDs) to provide trauma- and
abuse-informed care to those who access its services. Abuse erodes safety and trust; thus, a core
principle of our work is to enable people and communities affected by abuse to feel physically and
emotionally safe over the course of the research. We know that historically research has been extractive
especially in racialised and marginalised populations, and it can lead to re-traumatisation in the context
of gender-based violence and other traumas (e.g. racial discrimination and abuse). Therefore, every
opportunity must be taken to avoid causing harm to the people and communities we work with – as
participants, NHS personnel, advisors and knowledge users. With our goal to develop trauma-informed
practice in the ED, it is vital we model best practice in safety, as a pi

secppi__001_b.text:
llar of this practice. Our approach to
safety (see Box 3 in Detailed Research Plan) sets out measures for ensuring the welfare and wellbeing
of all involved.
(2) This project is rooted in participatory action research. Working with people and communities affected
by the research is fully embedded across all stages—from design of the research to dissemination—to
ensure the research is safe, relevant, responsive and meaningful to survivors. Survivors’ Voices
(represented by co-applicant AN) is a national, survivor-led organisation with expertise in enabling the
involvement of survivors in research through the best practice frameworks it has developed. The
researchers have previously collaborated with the team at Survivors Voices who, for the current project,
proposed a programme of work that will train our staff, coordinate lived experience input, shape and
support recruitment of survivors, intervention development, creative activities/outputs, data analysis,
knowledge-sharing, and crucially, provide peer support to any participant with lived experience who
would value it (see Working in partnership with those affected by your research, in Detailed Research
Plan).
(3) Survivors of ab

secppi__001_c.text:
use are not a homogeneous group; and their needs are shaped by intersecting
identities such as class, ethnicity, gender, and disability. Survivors from marginalised groups face
disproportionate barriers to care and support. Abuse can aggravate existing vulnerabilities, social
isolation, inequalities and put people at risk of further violent victimisation. Previous research shows that
people with mental health issues, insecure housing, cultural minorities, and migrant women are among
the most excluded from early care. A priority of our work is ensuring the co-designed ED intervention is
fit for purpose - that it responds to all survivors and supports ED staff to meet the needs of the most
underserved. Achieving this demands that we represent a wide range of survivor voices. We will engage
an Inclusion team (led by a survivor-activist and leader) to ensure fair distribution of power, voice and
knowledge, using the funding to monitor and tackle barriers to participation of survivors of diverse and
marginalised communities (see Inclusive research, in Detailed Research Plan).

evidence.text:
Research question
What are the components of an intervention to embed trauma-informed practices responsive to the
needs of survivors of sexual violence and abuse in emergency departments (EDs)?
Background
Sexual violence has lifelong impacts on survivors’ physical and mental health, is associated with
significant health inequalities and very costly to society. Survivors often avoid healthcare (e.g. cancer
screening) due to prior negative encounters with services and fears about re-traumatisation. EDs are a
vital entry point to the NHS, but the setting (e.g. urgency of care, fast-paced environment) creates
significant access challenges for trauma survivors. Among the 25 million annual ED attendances in
England, over 4m are estimated to be survivors of lifetime sexual violence. Current ED practices may
aggravate distress, self-discharge, and poor adherence to advice. Staff report feeling underprepared to
support survivors. Trauma-informed care can transform the healthcare encounter by prioritising safety,
trust, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. However, there is limited evidence on introducing these
practices into EDs.

, trauma-informed intervention to improve ED care for survivors
of sexual trauma.
Objectives:
1. Examine lived experiences of survivors accessing ED care and staff who provide the care and
prioritise what needs to change at the patient-clinician interface, and for health professionals, the
environment and organisation.
2. Identify ‘functions’ of the planned intervention
3. Co-design intervention components
4. Develop an underpinning theory of change.
Methods
Integrating Experience-Based Co-Design with a behaviour change framework, survivors and staff will
come together to co-design an intervention. Arts-based methods will underpin discovery, co-produced
content and knowledge transfer. Partner organisation, Survivors Voices, will lead on work with people
and communities, provide peer support and expertly facilitate co-design groups to engage survivors.
Phase 1 M1-16 Identify the need for change using focus groups with NHS staff (n=20), and narrative
interviews with survivors (n=30) recruited in health and community. We will gather narratives and
‘touchpoints’ of ED care to form the basis of arts-based outputs and prioritise targets for change.

evidence.text:
Research question
What are the components of an intervention to embed trauma-informed practices responsive to the
needs of survivors of sexual violence and abuse in emergency departments (EDs)?
Background
Sexual violence has lifelong impacts on survivors’ physical and mental health, is associated with
significant health inequalities and very costly to society. Survivors often avoid healthcare (e.g. cancer
screening) due to prior negative encounters with services and fears about re-traumatisation. EDs are a
vital entry point to the NHS, but the setting (e.g. urgency of care, fast-paced environment) creates
significant access challenges for trauma survivors. Among the 25 million annual ED attendances in
England, over 4m are estimated to be survivors of lifetime sexual violence. Current ED practices may
aggravate distress, self-discharge, and poor adherence to advice. Staff report feeling underprepared to
support survivors. Trauma-informed care can transform the healthcare encounter by prioritising safety,
trust, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. However, there is limited evidence on introducing these
practices into EDs.

, trauma-informed intervention to improve ED care for survivors
of sexual trauma.
Objectives:
1. Examine lived experiences of survivors accessing ED care and staff who provide the care and
prioritise what needs to change at the patient-clinician interface, and for health professionals, the
environment and organisation.
2. Identify ‘functions’ of the planned intervention
3. Co-design intervention components
4. Develop an underpinning theory of change.
Methods
Integrating Experience-Based Co-Design with a behaviour change framework, survivors and staff will
come together to co-design an intervention. Arts-based methods will underpin discovery, co-produced
content and knowledge transfer. Partner organisation, Survivors Voices, will lead on work with people
and communities, provide peer support and expertly facilitate co-design groups to engage survivors.
Phase 1 M1-16 Identify the need for change using focus groups with NHS staff (n=20), and narrative
interviews with survivors (n=30) recruited in health and community. We will gather narratives and
‘touchpoints’ of ED care to form the basis of arts-based outputs and prioritise targets for change.

evidence.text:
What’s this study about?
This study is about improving emergency department care for people who have experienced sexual
violence and abuse.
Why it matters
Sexual violence causes deep and lasting harm to survivors, their families, and communities. More than
10 million people in Britain have experienced sexual violence as children or adults. Sexual violence
affects physical, sexual, and mental health. But visiting the GP, having screening tests or attending
hospital can feel uncomfortable, even distressing for survivors. Survivors may worry about not having a
say in what happens to them or their bodies, being dismissed or blamed for their difficulties. They may
re-live feelings or memories connected with abuse. Many survivors avoid healthcare altogether which
can make health worse.
Emergency departments are especially difficult for survivors – the care is urgent and unavoidable. The
environment is busy, noisy, and very clinical. Emergency departments could be more survivor-centred –
like offering greater choice, showing care in asking about abuse, or for consent before examinations or
treatment, and taking steps to help a person feel safe. These changes could improve fairness and
acc

What this study will do?
We will plan a new approach to care for survivors. We will do this using a type of research called
‘Experience-Based Co-Design’. Survivors will be invited to share about times they went to the
emergency department. NHS staff will tell about caring for survivors as patients. Using what survivors
and staff tell us, we will work with survivor-artists to create powerful outputs, including a short film. Next,
survivors and staff will work together to agree goals for the new approach for emergency departments.
For example, a goal might be to improve NHS staff understanding about the health impacts of sexual
violence or create a safer environment. Finally, smaller groups of participants will plan how to make
changes, like showing staff how to communicate concerns about abuse.

survivors from a range of communities, safely and fairly. They advised that arts-based methods are an

Research question
What are the components of an intervention to embed trauma-informed practices responsive to the
needs of survivors of sexual violence and abuse in emergency departments (EDs)?
Background
Sexual violence has lifelong impacts on survivors’ physical and mental health, is associated with
significant health inequalities and very costly to society. Survivors often avoid healthcare (e.g. cancer
screening) due to prior negative encounters with services and fears about re-traumatisation. EDs are a
vital entry point to the NHS, but the setting (e.g. urgency of care, fast-paced environment) creates
significant access challenges for trauma survivors. Among the 25 million annual ED attendances in
England, over 4m are estimated to be survivors of lifetime sexual violence. Current ED practices may
aggravate distress, self-discharge, and poor adherence to advice. Staff report feeling underprepared to
support survivors. Trauma-informed care can transform the healthcare encounter by prioritising safety,
trust, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. However, there is limited evidence on introducing these
practices into EDs.

evidence.text:
, trauma-informed intervention to improve ED care for survivors
of sexual trauma.
Objectives:
1. Examine lived experiences of survivors accessing ED care and staff who provide the care and
prioritise what needs to change at the patient-clinician interface, and for health professionals, the
environment and organisation.
2. Identify ‘functions’ of the planned intervention
3. Co-design intervention components
4. Develop an underpinning theory of change.
Methods
Integrating Experience-Based Co-Design with a behaviour change framework, survivors and staff will
come together to co-design an intervention. Arts-based methods will underpin discovery, co-produced
content and knowledge transfer. Partner organisation, Survivors Voices, will lead on work with people
and communities, provide peer support and expertly facilitate co-design groups to engage survivors.
Phase 1 M1-16 Identify the need for change using focus groups with NHS staff (n=20), and narrative
interviews with survivors (n=30) recruited in health and community. We will gather narratives and
‘touchpoints’ of ED care to form the basis of arts-based outputs and prioritise targets for change.
Phase 2 M17-18 Define intervention func

tions using a co-design event with a mixed subset of 20
participants.
Phase 3 M19-24 Co-develop prototypes for the intervention in 3 mixed sub-groups with 15 participants,
such as skills training, resources tailored to the ED, local agreed referral pathways.
Impact and mobilisation
Findings to be shared widely in accessible formats with community and survivor organisations and key
professional bodies e.g. RCEM and RCN. This work will provide a foundation for quality improvement,
healthcare policy, ED service design, NHS efficiencies and better health and healthcare for survivors.
We will refine a theory of change and prepare for the next stage, to address uncertainties and
demonstrate promise in a feasibility study.
Inclusion
Boosting our work with survivors and communities, an inclusion strategy will expand reach to
underserved communities at the intersection of a high prevalence of sexual violence, poor health and
marginalisation. Actions include geographically and demographically diverse NHS sites, local
champions, and bespoke support for participation.

(ii) Outline number/type of sites involved
There will be 2 socio-economically differentiated sites: University Hospitals Coventry & Warwickshire
NHS Trust (UHCW) and South Warwickshire University NHS Foundation Trust (SWFT). Both sites are
Type 1 EDs - one is a major trauma centre (UHCW) and one is a local emergency hospital (SWFT).

Recognising that outcomes are shaped by complex, interacting factors across a complex healthcare
system, and consistent with existing research on transformative care for abuse, the intervention would
target change at the levels of patient experience, healthcare professionals’ practice, environment and
systems.
(iv) Address concerns about feasibility and sustainability of the proposed approach, given staff turnover
and environment in EDs

evidence.text:
We thank the committee for their review and constructive comments. Please find below responses to the
points raised.
1. The rationale of proposed study was unclear...clarify how arts-based and creative outputs would feed
into intervention development and what final intervention might be.
We included stronger rationale based on gap in evidence for transforming EDs from places of distress
into environments of healing and empowerment for survivors, and a policy impetus to reshape EDs for
better sexual violence and mental health care in the future.
Arts-based methods are central to the participatory approach needed to achieve the aims. Endorsed by
PPI, creative outputs (e.g. 5-min film reflecting stakeholders’ needs-in-context) register the collective
effort of staff and survivors. They feed into intervention development by 1) stimulating co-design work 2)
being integrated into the intervention (e.g. to impart skills, educate, stimulate action) and 3) sharing key
messages in ways that engage and are accessible.
The final intervention is based on priorities identified, existing theory/evidence and the co-designed
functions/features. It will include elements to target change at the patie

particularly if participants would be self-identifying as survivors

evidence.text:
What’s this study about?
This study is about improving emergency department care for people who have experienced sexual
violence and abuse.
Why it matters
Sexual violence causes deep and lasting harm to survivors, their families, and communities. More than
10 million people in Britain have experienced sexual violence as children or adults. Sexual violence
affects physical, sexual, and mental health. But visiting the GP, having screening tests or attending
hospital can feel uncomfortable, even distressing for survivors. Survivors may worry about not having a
say in what happens to them or their bodies, being dismissed or blamed for their difficulties. They may
re-live feelings or memories connected with abuse. Many survivors avoid healthcare altogether which
can make health worse.
Emergency departments are especially difficult for survivors – the care is urgent and unavoidable. The
environment is busy, noisy, and very clinical. Emergency departments could be more survivor-centred –
like offering greater choice, showing care in asking about abuse, or for consent before examinations or
treatment, and taking steps to help a person feel safe. These changes could improve fairness and
acc

evidence.text:
Research question
What are the components of an intervention to embed trauma-informed practices responsive to the
needs of survivors of sexual violence and abuse in emergency departments (EDs)?
Background
Sexual violence has lifelong impacts on survivors’ physical and mental health, is associated with
significant health inequalities and very costly to society. Survivors often avoid healthcare (e.g. cancer
screening) due to prior negative encounters with services and fears about re-traumatisation. EDs are a
vital entry point to the NHS, but the setting (e.g. urgency of care, fast-paced environment) creates
significant access challenges for trauma survivors. Among the 25 million annual ED attendances in
England, over 4m are estimated to be survivors of lifetime sexual violence. Current ED practices may
aggravate distress, self-discharge, and poor adherence to advice. Staff report feeling underprepared to
support survivors. Trauma-informed care can transform the healthcare encounter by prioritising safety,
trust, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. However, there is limited evidence on introducing these
practices into EDs.
Aims and objectives
This study aims to co-design a complex

Objectives:
1. Examine lived experiences of survivors accessing ED care and staff who provide the care and
prioritise what needs to change at the patient-clinician interface, and for health professionals, the
environment and organisation.
2. Identify ‘functions’ of the planned intervention
3. Co-design intervention components
4. Develop an underpinning theory of change.
Methods
Integrating Experience-Based Co-Design with a behaviour change framework, survivors and staff will
come together to co-design an intervention. Arts-based methods will underpin discovery, co-produced
content and knowledge transfer. Partner organisation, Survivors Voices, will lead on work with people
and communities, provide peer support and expertly facilitate co-design groups to engage survivors.
Phase 1 M1-16 Identify the need for change using focus groups with NHS staff (n=20), and narrative
interviews with survivors (n=30) recruited in health and community. We will gather narratives and
‘touchpoints’ of ED care to form the basis of arts-based outputs and prioritise targets for change.

participants.
Phase 3 M19-24 Co-develop prototypes for the intervention in 3 mixed sub-groups with 15 participants,
such as skills training, resources tailored to the ED, local agreed referral pathways.
Impact and mobilisation
Findings to be shared widely in accessible formats with community and survivor organisations and key
professional bodies e.g. RCEM and RCN. This work will provide a foundation for quality improvement,
healthcare policy, ED service design, NHS efficiencies and better health and healthcare for survivors.
We will refine a theory of change and prepare for the next stage, to address uncertainties and
demonstrate promise in a feasibility study.
Inclusion
Boosting our work with survivors and communities, an inclusion strategy will expand reach to
underserved communities at the intersection of a high prevalence of sexual violence, poor health and
marginalisation. Actions include geographically and demographically diverse NHS sites, local

evidence.text:
Research question
What are the components of an intervention to embed trauma-informed practices responsive to the
needs of survivors of sexual violence and abuse in emergency departments (EDs)?
Background
Sexual violence has lifelong impacts on survivors’ physical and mental health, is associated with
significant health inequalities and very costly to society. Survivors often avoid healthcare (e.g. cancer
screening) due to prior negative encounters with services and fears about re-traumatisation. EDs are a
vital entry point to the NHS, but the setting (e.g. urgency of care, fast-paced environment) creates
significant access challenges for trauma survivors. Among the 25 million annual ED attendances in
England, over 4m are estimated to be survivors of lifetime sexual violence. Current ED practices may
aggravate distress, self-discharge, and poor adherence to advice. Staff report feeling underprepared to
support survivors. Trauma-informed care can transform the healthcare encounter by prioritising safety,
trust, choice, collaboration, and empowerment. However, there is limited evidence on introducing these
practices into EDs.
Aims and objectives
This study aims to co-design a complex

tions using a co-design event with a mixed subset of 20
participants.
Phase 3 M19-24 Co-develop prototypes for the intervention in 3 mixed sub-groups with 15 participants,
such as skills training, resources tailored to the ED, local agreed referral pathways.
Impact and mobilisation
Findings to be shared widely in accessible formats with community and survivor organisations and key
professional bodies e.g. RCEM and RCN. This work will provide a foundation for quality improvement,
healthcare policy, ED service design, NHS efficiencies and better health and healthcare for survivors.
We will refine a theory of change and prepare for the next stage, to address uncertainties and
demonstrate promise in a feasibility study.
Inclusion
Boosting our work with survivors and communities, an inclusion strategy will expand reach to
underserved communities at the intersection of a high prevalence of sexual violence, poor health and
marginalisation. Actions include geographically and demographically diverse NHS sites, local
champions, and bespoke support for participation.

What’s this study about?
This study is about improving emergency department care for people who have experienced sexual
violence and abuse.
Why it matters
Sexual violence causes deep and lasting harm to survivors, their families, and communities. More than
10 million people in Britain have experienced sexual violence as children or adults. Sexual violence
affects physical, sexual, and mental health. But visiting the GP, having screening tests or attending
hospital can feel uncomfortable, even distressing for survivors. Survivors may worry about not having a
say in what happens to them or their bodies, being dismissed or blamed for their difficulties. They may
re-live feelings or memories connected with abuse. Many survivors avoid healthcare altogether which
can make health worse.
Emergency departments are especially difficult for survivors – the care is urgent and unavoidable. The
environment is busy, noisy, and very clinical. Emergency departments could be more survivor-centred –
like offering greater choice, showing care in asking about abuse, or for consent before examinations or
treatment, and taking steps to help a person feel safe. These changes could improve fairness and
acc
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
o-lead this work.
What will we do with the results?
We will set out a new approach to emergency care that allows survivors get the healthcare they need.
We will plan a follow-on study, looking at whether the approach could lead to better outcomes for
survivors, staff and the NHS. We will explore if it might be possible and sensible to roll-out to emergency
departments nationwide in the future. Creative outputs will explain what we did and learnt.

llar of this practice. Our approach to
safety (see Box 3 in Detailed Research Plan) sets out measures for ensuring the welfare and wellbeing
of all involved.
(2) This project is rooted in participatory action research. Working with people and communities affected
by the research is fully embedded across all stages—from design of the research to dissemination—to
ensure the research is safe, relevant, responsive and meaningful to survivors. Survivors’ Voices
(represented by co-applicant AN) is a national, survivor-led organisation with expertise in enabling the
involvement of survivors in research through the best practice frameworks it has developed. The
researchers have previously collaborated with the team at Survivors Voices who, for the current project,
proposed a programme of work that will train our staff, coordinate lived experience input, shape and
support recruitment of survivors, intervention development, creative activities/outputs, data analysis,
knowledge-sharing, and crucially, provide peer support to any participant with lived experience who
would value it (see Working in partnership with those affected by your research, in Detailed Research
Plan).
(3) Survivors of ab

use are not a homogeneous group; and their needs are shaped by intersecting
identities such as class, ethnicity, gender, and disability. Survivors from marginalised groups face
disproportionate barriers to care and support. Abuse can aggravate existing vulnerabilities, social
isolation, inequalities and put people at risk of further violent victimisation. Previous research shows that
people with mental health issues, insecure housing, cultural minorities, and migrant women are among
the most excluded from early care. A priority of our work is ensuring the co-designed ED intervention is
fit for purpose - that it responds to all survivors and supports ED staff to meet the needs of the most
underserved. Achieving this demands that we represent a wide range of survivor voices. We will engage
an Inclusion team (led by a survivor-activist and leader) to ensure fair distribution of power, voice and
knowledge, using the funding to monitor and tackle barriers to participation of survivors of diverse and
marginalised communities (see Inclusive research, in Detailed Research Plan).

Impact and mobilisation
Findings to be shared widely in accessible formats with community and survivor organisations and key
professional bodies e.g. RCEM and RCN. This work will provide a foundation for quality improvement,
healthcare policy, ED service design, NHS efficiencies and better health and healthcare for survivors.
We will refine a theory of change and prepare for the next stage, to address uncertainties and
demonstrate promise in a feasibility study.
Inclusion
Boosting our work with survivors and communities, an inclusion strategy will expand reach to
underserved communities at the intersection of a high prevalence of sexual violence, poor health and
marginalisation. Actions include geographically and demographically diverse NHS sites, local
champions, and bespoke support for participation.

What this study will do?
We will plan a new approach to care for survivors. We will do this using a type of research called
‘Experience-Based Co-Design’. Survivors will be invited to share about times they went to the
emergency department. NHS staff will tell about caring for survivors as patients. Using what survivors
and staff tell us, we will work with survivor-artists to create powerful outputs, including a short film. Next,
survivors and staff will work together to agree goals for the new approach for emergency departments.
For example, a goal might be to improve NHS staff understanding about the health impacts of sexual
violence or create a safer environment. Finally, smaller groups of participants will plan how to make
changes, like showing staff how to communicate concerns about abuse.
Survivors and community leaders were involved in planning the research to date, especially on including
survivors from a range of communities, safely and fairly. They advised that arts-based methods are an
excellent way to share about research and that health services can be places of healing over distress. It

evidence.text:
use are not a homogeneous group; and their needs are shaped by intersecting
identities such as class, ethnicity, gender, and disability. Survivors from marginalised groups face
disproportionate barriers to care and support. Abuse can aggravate existing vulnerabilities, social
isolation, inequalities and put people at risk of further violent victimisation. Previous research shows that
people with mental health issues, insecure housing, cultural minorities, and migrant women are among
the most excluded from early care. A priority of our work is ensuring the co-designed ED intervention is
fit for purpose - that it responds to all survivors and supports ED staff to meet the needs of the most
underserved. Achieving this demands that we represent a wide range of survivor voices. We will engage
an Inclusion team (led by a survivor-activist and leader) to ensure fair distribution of power, voice and
knowledge, using the funding to monitor and tackle barriers to participation of survivors of diverse and
marginalised communities (see Inclusive research, in Detailed Research Plan).

, trauma-informed intervention to improve ED care for survivors
of sexual trauma.
Objectives:
1. Examine lived experiences of survivors accessing ED care and staff who provide the care and
prioritise what needs to change at the patient-clinician interface, and for health professionals, the
environment and organisation.
2. Identify ‘functions’ of the planned intervention
3. Co-design intervention components
4. Develop an underpinning theory of change.
Methods
Integrating Experience-Based Co-Design with a behaviour change framework, survivors and staff will
come together to co-design an intervention. Arts-based methods will underpin discovery, co-produced
content and knowledge transfer. Partner organisation, Survivors Voices, will lead on work with people
and communities, provide peer support and expertly facilitate co-design groups to engage survivors.
Phase 1 M1-16 Identify the need for change using focus groups with NHS staff (n=20), and narrative
interviews with survivors (n=30) recruited in health and community. We will gather narratives and
‘touchpoints’ of ED care to form the basis of arts-based outputs and prioritise targets for change.
Phase 2 M17-18 Define intervention func

essibility, survivors’ health and lower NHS costs.
What this study will do?
We will plan a new approach to care for survivors. We will do this using a type of research called
‘Experience-Based Co-Design’. Survivors will be invited to share about times they went to the
emergency department. NHS staff will tell about caring for survivors as patients. Using what survivors
and staff tell us, we will work with survivor-artists to create powerful outputs, including a short film. Next,
survivors and staff will work together to agree goals for the new approach for emergency departments.
For example, a goal might be to improve NHS staff understanding about the health impacts of sexual
violence or create a safer environment. Finally, smaller groups of participants will plan how to make
changes, like showing staff how to communicate concerns about abuse.
Survivors and community leaders were involved in planning the research to date, especially on including
survivors from a range of communities, safely and fairly. They advised that arts-based methods are an
excellent way to share about research and that health services can be places of healing over distress. It

use are not a homogeneous group; and their needs are shaped by intersecting
identities such as class, ethnicity, gender, and disability. Survivors from marginalised groups face
disproportionate barriers to care and support. Abuse can aggravate existing vulnerabilities, social
isolation, inequalities and put people at risk of further violent victimisation. Previous research shows that
people with mental health issues, insecure housing, cultural minorities, and migrant women are among
the most excluded from early care. A priority of our work is ensuring the co-designed ED intervention is
fit for purpose - that it responds to all survivors and supports ED staff to meet the needs of the most
underserved. Achieving this demands that we represent a wide range of survivor voices. We will engage
an Inclusion team (led by a survivor-activist and leader) to ensure fair distribution of power, voice and
knowledge, using the funding to monitor and tackle barriers to participation of survivors of diverse and
marginalised communities (see Inclusive research, in Detailed Research Plan).

evidence.text:
, trauma-informed intervention to improve ED care for survivors
of sexual trauma.
Objectives:
1. Examine lived experiences of survivors accessing ED care and staff who provide the care and
prioritise what needs to change at the patient-clinician interface, and for health professionals, the
environment and organisation.
2. Identify ‘functions’ of the planned intervention
3. Co-design intervention components
4. Develop an underpinning theory of change.
Methods
Integrating Experience-Based Co-Design with a behaviour change framework, survivors and staff will
come together to co-design an intervention. Arts-based methods will underpin discovery, co-produced
content and knowledge transfer. Partner organisation, Survivors Voices, will lead on work with people
and communities, provide peer support and expertly facilitate co-design groups to engage survivors.
Phase 1 M1-16 Identify the need for change using focus groups with NHS staff (n=20), and narrative
interviews with survivors (n=30) recruited in health and community. We will gather narratives and
‘touchpoints’ of ED care to form the basis of arts-based outputs and prioritise targets for change.
Phase 2 M17-18 Define intervention func

essibility, survivors’ health and lower NHS costs.
What this study will do?
We will plan a new approach to care for survivors. We will do this using a type of research called
‘Experience-Based Co-Design’. Survivors will be invited to share about times they went to the
emergency department. NHS staff will tell about caring for survivors as patients. Using what survivors
and staff tell us, we will work with survivor-artists to create powerful outputs, including a short film. Next,
survivors and staff will work together to agree goals for the new approach for emergency departments.
For example, a goal might be to improve NHS staff understanding about the health impacts of sexual
violence or create a safer environment. Finally, smaller groups of participants will plan how to make
changes, like showing staff how to communicate concerns about abuse.
Survivors and community leaders were involved in planning the research to date, especially on including
survivors from a range of communities, safely and fairly. They advised that arts-based methods are an
excellent way to share about research and that health services can be places of healing over distress. It

Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly mentions advice from community leaders on including survivors from a range of communities safely and fairly.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes survivors co-leading the work and using arts-based methods to shape the research design and outputs.
Plain English Summary of Research: Plans to use creative outputs to explain findings and explore nationwide rollout, maximizing public benefit.

Plain English Summary of Research: Ensures inclusivity across the lifecycle by involving survivors in planning, design, and output creation.
